title: Hall conductivity reveals the nature of quantum coherence in strongly correlated metals
authors: Emily Z. Zhang, Thomas P. Devereaux
abstract: Linear-in-temperature resistivity is a hallmark for strange metallic transport, and appears universally in many strongly correlated electron systems. However, the focus on the longitudinal channel often overshadows the profound microscopic insights contained within the transverse response. Here, we utilize numerically exact determinantal quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the doped Hubbard model in a magnetic field to calculate longitudinal and transverse transport. We demonstrate that while the resistivity is robustly $T$-linear across parameter sets, the Hall response is highly sensitive to particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi surface topology, and many-body correlation effects. Specifically, the combination of these effects determine a crossover scale in which the system becomes quantum-coherent, and is reflected in the Hall conductivity. Our results demonstrate that while the $T$-linearity in resistivity appears universal, the Hall response reveals a crossover from semi-classical to quantum-coherent transport otherwise masked in the longitudinal channel.
summary: This work investigates quantum coherence in strongly correlated metals by comparing longitudinal and transverse electrical responses. Using advanced Quantum Monte Carlo simulations on the doped Hubbard model, the authors show that while resistance appears universally temperature-linear, the Hall conductivity reveals a distinct crossover scale marking the transition to true quantum coherence. This highlights $\sigma_{xy}$ as a superior diagnostic tool for probing underlying many-body physics.
main_problem: To understand the nature of quantum coherence in strongly correlated metals by investigating transport properties beyond simple quasiparticle descriptions.
main_result: While longitudinal resistivity remains robustly linear in temperature, the Hall conductivity reveals a crossover from semi-classical to quantum-coherent transport that is otherwise masked in the resistivity measurement.
method: The study employs numerically exact determinantal Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) simulations and high-temperature expansions of current-current correlators to calculate both longitudinal and transverse transport coefficients.
model_or_system: The research focuses on strongly correlated electron systems, specifically utilizing the doped Hubbard model with next-nearest-neighbor hopping ($t'$).
key_observables: Longitudinal resistivity ($ho_{xx}$), Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), and the average double occupancy $\langle d angle$.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), doping level, particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi surface topology, and hopping parameters ($t'$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on comparing results from DQMC simulations with high-temperature expansions of conductivities, and sometimes uses proxy methods for DC conductivity.
figures_summary: Figures compare the temperature dependence of $\cot(	heta_H)$, showing distinct power laws; they contrast robustly linear $ho_{xx}$ with qualitatively different $R_H$ behavior across varying parameters.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares longitudinal and transverse transport responses, arguing that the Hall response ($\sigma_{xy}$) is uniquely sensitive to correlation-induced spectral weight rearrangement and the onset of quantum coherence, unlike the resistivity ($ho$).
why_interesting: The focus on $\sigma_{xy}$ as a probe for coherence mechanisms (related to Aharonov-Bohm phases) provides an alternative diagnostic tool compared to standard transport measurements like resistivity.
